rationale: The citing paper uses Khan, Zhu, and Kalra (2011) in its theory development to support a core premise: high (vs. low) representation reduces attribute-level trade-offs and shifts choices toward high-level preferences. This premise is directly tied to the paper's proposed mechanism for why abstract versus concrete representation changes the difficulty of comparable versus noncomparable choices. The cited paper is also used in the discussion to justify the claim that consumers' default mindset tends to be relatively concrete. These are functional, mechanism-level uses rather than a passing literature-list mention.
relation_description: The 2013 paper treats the 2011 paper as prior evidence that construal level changes trade-off processing and preference focus. It builds its own choice-difficulty framework on that mechanism and uses the 2011 finding to interpret why noncomparable choices are often harder under default (more concrete) processing.
